subject: science

primary.justification: The chapter's central mission is using the particulate model — constituent particles, interparticle attractions, interparticle spacing, and particle motion — to represent the properties of matter and explain state changes (melting point, boiling point). The phrase 'particulate nature to represent properties of matter' is essentially the chapter's organising principle, addressed across every section from 7.1 through 7.4.

The chapter systematically classifies matter into three physical states (solid, liquid, gas) by their observable properties — definite/indefinite shape, definite/indefinite volume, compressibility — through the comparative activities in sections 7.2 and 7.3, and consolidates this in the summary table. This classification strand is architecturally distinct from the particulate-representation strand of C-1.2.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to explain the observable properties and state-changes of matter by using the particulate model — constituent particles, interparticle attractions, interparticle spacing, and particle motion — to represent what is happening at the submicroscopic level in solids, liquids, and gases.
